section_summaries.heading: Artificial Intelligence
section_summaries.summary: Lecture 1 - Introduction School of Information and Communication Technology - HUST

section_summaries.heading: Outline
section_summaries.summary: - What is AI? - Foundations of AI - Short history of AI - Philosophical discussions

section_summaries.heading: What is AI?
section_summaries.summary: Views of AI fall into four categories: | | | |---|---| | Think like humans | Thinking rationally | | Act like humans | Acting rationally | The textbook advocates "acting rationally"

section_summaries.heading: Think like humans
section_summaries.summary: - 1960s "cognitive revolution": information-processing psychology - Scientific theories of internal activities of the brain - What level of abstraction? “Knowledge” or “circuits”?

section_summaries.heading: Act like humans
section_summaries.summary: - Turing (1950) "Computing machinery and intelligence": - "Can machines think?" → "Can machines behave intelligently?" - Operational test for intelligent behavior: the Imitation Game [Diagram: The Imitation Game frames intelligence as observable behavior in a conversational...

section_summaries.heading: Thinking rationally
section_summaries.summary: - The “Laws of Thought” approach - What does it mean to “think rationally”? - Normative / prescriptive rather than descriptive - Logicist tradition: - Logic: notation and rules of derivation for thoughts - Aristotle: what are correct arguments/thought processes?

section_summaries.heading: Acting rationally
section_summaries.summary: - Rational behavior: doing the “right thing” - The right thing: that which is expected to maximize goal achievement, given the available information - Doesn't necessarily involve thinking, e.g., blinking - Thinking can be in the service of rational action - Entirely dependent...

section_summaries.heading: Rational agents
section_summaries.summary: - An **agent** is an entity that perceives and acts - An agent function maps from percept histories to actions: $$\mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$ [Diagram: The figure shows an agent as an interface between sensing and acting in an environment. It emphasizes that...

section_summaries.heading: Foundations of AI
section_summaries.summary: - Philosophy: logic, methods of reasoning, mind as physical system foundations of learning, language, rationality - Mathematics: formal representation and proof algorithms, computation, (un)decidability, (in)tractability, probability - Economics: utility, decision theory -...

section_summaries.heading: Short history of AI
section_summaries.summary: - 1940-1950: Early days - 1943: McCulloch & Pitts: Boolean circuit model of brain - 1950: Turing's ``Computing Machinery and Intelligence“ - 1950—70: Excitement: Look, Ma, no hands! - 1950s: Early AI programs, including Samuel's checkers program, Newell & Simon's Logic...

section_summaries.heading: Expert system
section_summaries.summary: [Diagram: The slide contrasts a conventional computer system with an expert system. The key idea is that expert systems combine human expertise with inference/reasoning, and that knowledge engineering is the process of capturing that expertise so the system can produce useful...

section_summaries.heading: State of the art
section_summaries.summary: - May, '97: Deep Blue vs. Kasparov - First match won against world-champion - “Intelligent creative" play - 200 million board positions per second!

section_summaries.heading: Philosophical discussions
section_summaries.summary: What Can AI Do? - Play a decent game of table tennis?

section_summaries.heading: Some problems with AI
section_summaries.summary: - People might lose their jobs to automation. - People might have too much (or too little) leisure time.
